concepts.name: Types of Solutions
concepts.explanation: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures of two or more components. The component present in largest quantity is the solvent, and others are solutes. Solutions can be gaseous, liquid, or solid depending on the physical state of the solvent.

concepts.name: Mass Percentage (w/w)
concepts.explanation: Mass percentage is the mass of the component divided by the total mass of the solution, multiplied by 100. It is commonly used in industrial applications.
concepts.formula_reaction: Mass % = (Mass of component / Total mass of solution) × 100

concepts.name: Volume Percentage (v/v)
concepts.explanation: Volume percentage is the volume of the component divided by the total volume of the solution, multiplied by 100. It is commonly used for liquid-liquid solutions.
concepts.formula_reaction: Volume % = (Volume of component / Total volume of solution) × 100

concepts.name: Mass by Volume Percentage (w/v)
concepts.explanation: Mass by volume percentage is the mass of solute dissolved in 100 mL of solution. It is commonly used in medicine and pharmacy.
concepts.formula_reaction: Mass by volume % = (Mass of solute / Volume of solution in mL) × 100

concepts.name: Parts Per Million (ppm)
concepts.explanation: Parts per million is used for expressing trace concentrations. It is the number of parts of the component per 10⁶ parts of the solution. It can be mass/mass, volume/volume, or mass/volume.
concepts.formula_reaction: ppm = (Number of parts of component / Total number of parts of all components) × 10⁶

concepts.name: Mole Fraction (x)
concepts.explanation: Mole fraction is the ratio of the number of moles of a component to the total number of moles of all components in the solution. The sum of all mole fractions in a solution is unity.

concepts.name: Molarity (M)
concepts.explanation: Molarity is the number of moles of solute dissolved in one litre of solution. It is temperature-dependent because volume changes with temperature.
concepts.formula_reaction: Molarity = Moles of solute / Volume of solution in litres

concepts.name: Molality (m)
concepts.explanation: Molality is the number of moles of solute dissolved in one kilogram of solvent. It is temperature-independent because mass does not change with temperature.
concepts.formula_reaction: Molality = Moles of solute / Mass of solvent in kg

concepts.name: Solubility of Solids in Liquids
concepts.explanation: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can be dissolved in a specified amount of solvent at a given temperature. 'Like dissolves like' – polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents, and non-polar solutes dissolve in non-polar solvents.

concepts.name: Effect of Temperature on Solubility of Solids
concepts.explanation: If dissolution is endothermic (Δ_solH > 0), solubility increases with temperature. If dissolution is exothermic (Δ_solH < 0), solubility decreases with temperature, according to Le Chatelier's principle.

concepts.name: Solubility of Gases in Liquids
concepts.explanation: Gases dissolve in liquids. The solubility depends on temperature and pressure. Solubility of gases decreases with increase in temperature (dissolution is exothermic) and increases with increase in pressure.

concepts.name: Henry's Law
concepts.explanation: Henry's law states that the partial pressure of a gas above a solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution. p = K_H · x, where K_H is Henry's law constant. Higher K_H means lower solubility.

concepts.name: Applications of Henry's Law
concepts.explanation: Henry's law explains: (i) CO₂ solubility in soft drinks under high pressure; (ii) bends in scuba divers (nitrogen bubbles due to pressure decrease); (iii) anoxia at high altitudes (low partial pressure of O₂ in blood).

concepts.name: Vapour Pressure of Liquid-Liquid Solutions
concepts.explanation: For a solution of two volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution (Raoult's law). The total vapour pressure is the sum of partial pressures.

concepts.name: Raoult's Law for Volatile Components
concepts.explanation: Raoult's law states that for a solution of volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution: p_i = p_i⁰ · x_i. For a binary solution, p_total = p₁⁰x₁ + p₂⁰x₂.

concepts.name: Raoult's Law as a Special Case of Henry's Law
concepts.explanation: Raoult's law is a special case of Henry's law for a volatile liquid. In Henry's law, the proportionality constant is K_H; in Raoult's law, the proportionality constant is p_i⁰ (vapour pressure of the pure component).

concepts.name: Raoult's Law for Non-Volatile Solute (Relative Lowering of Vapour Pressure)
concepts.explanation: When a non-volatile solute is added to a solvent, the vapour pressure of the solvent decreases. The relative lowering of vapour pressure is equal to the mole fraction of the solute: (p₁⁰ - p₁)/p₁⁰ = x₂.

concepts.name: Ideal Solutions
concepts.explanation: Ideal solutions obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration. For ideal solutions, Δ_mixH = 0 and Δ_mixV = 0. A- A, B- B, and A- B interactions are similar. Examples: n-hexane and n-heptane, benzene and toluene.

concepts.name: Non-Ideal Solutions – Positive Deviation
concepts.explanation: Positive deviation from Raoult's law occurs when A- B interactions are weaker than A- A and B- B interactions. This results in higher vapour pressure than predicted. Δ_mixH > 0. Examples: ethanol + acetone, carbon disulphide + acetone.

concepts.name: Non-Ideal Solutions – Negative Deviation
concepts.explanation: Negative deviation from Raoult's law occurs when A- B interactions are stronger than A- A and B- B interactions. This results in lower vapour pressure than predicted. Δ_mixH < 0. Examples: chloroform + acetone, phenol + aniline.

concepts.name: Azeotropes – Minimum and Maximum Boiling
concepts.explanation: Azeotropes are mixtures that have the same composition in liquid and vapour phases and boil at a constant temperature. Minimum boiling azeotropes show large positive deviation (e.g., ethanol-water, 95% ethanol). Maximum boiling azeotropes show large negative deviation (e.g., nitric acid-water, 68% HNO₃).

concepts.name: Colligative Properties – Introduction
concepts.explanation: Colligative properties depend only on the number of solute particles present in the solution, irrespective of their nature. The four colligative properties are: (1) relative lowering of vapour pressure, (2) elevation of boiling point, (3) depression of freezing point, and (4) osmotic pressure.

concepts.name: Relative Lowering of Vapour Pressure
concepts.explanation: The relative lowering of vapour pressure is (p₁⁰ - p₁)/p₁⁰ = x₂ (mole fraction of solute). For dilute solutions, (p₁⁰ - p₁)/p₁⁰ = n₂/n₁ = (w₂/M₂) / (w₁/M₁). This is used to determine the molar mass of the solute.

concepts.name: Elevation of Boiling Point (ΔT_b)
concepts.explanation: The boiling point of a solution is higher than that of the pure solvent. ΔT_b = T_b - T_b⁰ = K_b · m, where K_b is the molal elevation constant (ebullioscopic constant) and m is the molality of the solution.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔT_b = K_b · m; M₂ = (1000 × w₂ × K_b) / (ΔT_b × w₁)

concepts.name: Depression of Freezing Point (ΔT_f)
concepts.explanation: The freezing point of a solution is lower than that of the pure solvent. ΔT_f = T_f⁰ - T_f = K_f · m, where K_f is the molal depression constant (cryoscopic constant) and m is the molality of the solution.
concepts.formula_reaction: ΔT_f = K_f · m; M₂ = (K_f × w₂ × 1000) / (ΔT_f × w₁)

concepts.name: Osmosis and Osmotic Pressure (Π)
concepts.explanation: Osmosis is the spontaneous flow of solvent molecules from pure solvent to solution through a semi-permeable membrane. Osmotic pressure (Π) is the excess pressure that must be applied to the solution to prevent osmosis.

concepts.name: Osmotic Pressure – Determination of Molar Mass
concepts.explanation: For dilute solutions, Π = C·R·T = (n/V)·R·T. Thus, M₂ = (w₂·R·T) / (Π·V). This method is especially useful for determining molar masses of macromolecules (proteins, polymers) as it is measured at room temperature.
concepts.formula_reaction: Π = CRT; M₂ = (w₂ · R · T) / (Π · V)

concepts.name: Isotonic, Hypertonic, and Hypotonic Solutions
concepts.explanation: Isotonic solutions have the same osmotic pressure (e.g., 0.9% NaCl solution with blood cells). Hypertonic solutions have higher osmotic pressure (cells shrink). Hypotonic solutions have lower osmotic pressure (cells swell).

concepts.name: Reverse Osmosis and Water Purification
concepts.explanation: Reverse osmosis occurs when a pressure larger than osmotic pressure is applied to the solution, causing the pure solvent to flow out through the semi-permeable membrane. It is used for desalination of sea water.

concepts.name: Abnormal Molar Masses
concepts.explanation: When solutes dissociate (electrolytes) or associate (e.g., acetic acid in benzene), the experimentally determined molar mass differs from the normal value. Dissociation gives lower molar mass; association gives higher molar mass.

concepts.name: van't Hoff Factor (i)
concepts.explanation: The van't Hoff factor (i) corrects colligative properties for dissociation or association: i = Normal molar mass / Abnormal molar mass = Observed colligative property / Calculated colligative property = Total moles after association/dissociation / Moles before association/dissociation.
concepts.formula_reaction: i = (Observed ΔT_f) / (Calculated ΔT_f); ΔT_f = i · K_f · m; ΔT_b = i · K_b · m; Π = i · C · R · T

concepts.name: Determination of Degree of Dissociation or Association using i
concepts.explanation: For dissociation: i = 1 + (n - 1)α, where n is the number of ions formed per molecule and α is degree of dissociation. For association: i = 1 - (n - 1)α/n, where n is the number of molecules associated and α is degree of association.
